coredata.dc:title: Based on FRET to construct color-tunable ultralong lifetime room temperature phosphorescent carbon dots in aqueous solution
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Lu, Feng
Xu, Xinhuan
Zhu, Xingdong
Shen, Linxin
Wan, Weizheng
Hu, Min
coredata.dc:description: Room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) Carbon Dots have been capturing increasing attention in recent years, while building a general method to adjust the emission color of RTP carbon dots is still a big challenge. Herein we report a simple method that combine the carbon nanodots and dyes (R6G and DCF) in SiO2 nanosphere to get a series of multicolor RTP nanodots (CD@SiO2@dye) with long lifetime in aqueous solution. Leverage on chitosan quaternary ammonium as matrix and diethylenetriamine as N-doping resource to form a cross-linked skeleton as a luminescent center (namely CD), and a rigid network is formed by silica encapsulation (CD@SiO2) to restrict the non-radiative transition process to generate the phosphorescence. The CD-based composites, with 1.10 s green (503 nm) phosphorescence emission, serve as activator to stimulate the corresponding luminescence of organic dyes. Then, based on Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) process from CDs (as donor) to organic dyes (as acceptor) under UV excitation, the CD@SiO2@R6G emit ultra-long lifetime (1.13 s) orange-yellow (570 nm) afterglow, and CD@SiO2@DCF emit ultra-long lifetime (1.20 s) yellow-green afterglow (530 nm). Furthermore, it also achieves RTP colors control when the ratio of CDs and the dyes changes, the ratio of green emission and dye’s emission activated by CDs will gradually change as well. These kinds of materials keep the inherent advantages of low toxicity and luminous stability, and achieve adjustable RTP color in aqueous solution. Our research provides a strategy to synthesize water-soluble long-life RTP CDs with adjustable color and lifetime.

head.ce:title: Based on FRET to construct color-tunable ultralong lifetime room temperature phosphorescent carbon dots in aqueous solution

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: Feng Lu and Xinhuan Xu contribute equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Room temperature phosphorescent (RTP) Carbon Dots have been capturing increasing attention in recent years, while building a general method to adjust the emission color of RTP carbon dots is still a big challenge. Herein we report a simple method that combine the carbon nanodots and dyes (R6G and DCF) in SiO2 nanosphere to get a series of multicolor RTP nanodots (CD@SiO2@dye) with long lifetime in aqueous solution. Leverage on chitosan quaternary ammonium as matrix and diethylenetriamine as N-doping resource to form a cross-linked skeleton as a luminescent center (namely CD), and a rigid network is formed by silica encapsulation (CD@SiO2) to restrict the non-radiative transition process to generate the phosphorescence. The CD-based composites, with 1.10 s green (503 nm) phosphorescence emission, serve as activator to stimulate the corresponding luminescence of organic dyes. Then, based on Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) process from CDs (as donor) to organic dyes (as acceptor) under UV excitation, the CD@SiO2@R6G emit ultra-long lifetime (1.13 s) orange-yellow (570 nm) afterglow, and CD@SiO2@DCF emit ultra-long lifetime (1.20 s) yellow-green afterglow (530 nm). Furthermore, it also achieves RTP colors control when the ratio of CDs and the dyes changes, the ratio of green emission and dye’s emission activated by CDs will gradually change as well. These kinds of materials keep the inherent advantages of low toxicity and luminous stability, and achieve adjustable RTP color in aqueous solution. Our research provides a strategy to synthesize water-soluble long-life RTP CDs with adjustable color and lifetime.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials, which can emit phosphorescence under excitation at room temperature, are superior to conventional luminous materials due to their high signal-to-noise ratio, long lifetime emission, background free and large Stokes shift properties [1–5]. Up to now, there are mainly two kinds of RTP materials: (1) Metal-based RTP materials; (2) Pure organic RTP materials. The metal-based RTP materials are usually designed with inorganic salt (as matrix) and transition or rare earth metals (as activators), possessing ultra-long lifetime, high luminous stability and tunable photophysical properties due to the multi-level transition of rare-earth metal elements [6–8]. However, metal-based RTP materials always accompany with high toxicity, high cost, complex synthesize route and poor processability, which partially limit their application in biological fields [9]. However, the pure organic room temperature phosphorescence (RTP) materials successfully overcome the disadvantages of metal-based RTP materials like high cost, biological toxicity, and environmental pollution. Nevertheless, pure organic RTP materials are relatively weak in luminous stability and short phosphorescence lifetime [10–12].

RTP carbon dots (RTP-CDs), differing from traditional RTP materials, are expected to be candidates for new prospective phosphorescent materials due to their excellent biocompatibility, low toxicity, photostability, and raw materials versatility [13–17]. Feng’s group designed a supramolecular cross-linked structure, which efficiently activated the phosphorescence emission of polymer carbon dots (PCDs) [18]. Furthermore, with the change of the supramolecular cross-linked structure, the emission color could be modulated as well, which was credit to the rigid structure inhibiting the non-radiative transition of luminescence center (namely CEE effect) [19]. Zhang’s group discovered that the CDs/polyvinyl alcohol matrix could emit bright phosphorescence through introducing the O,F-codopant, since the increasing content of C-O-C groups and the F-doping reduced the
Δ
E
st
in the system, which promoted the ISC (intersystem crossing) process and improved their phosphorescence performance [20]. Additionally, Su’s group synthesized a new kind of P-doping carbon dots, whose emission could exhibit a lifetime of 429.880 ms when coated on filter paper through hydrogen bonds. The hydrogen bonds formed between functional groups of CDs and matrix suppressed the non-radiative transition, reduced the energy loss, and elevated the phosphorescence intensity and lifetime remarkably [21]. The solid CDs-based RTP has made great progress in long lifetime, emission, and dynamic changes recently [6,8,16,17,22–27]. Sun’s group elevated the phosphorescence lifetime of carbon dots to the level of 5.72 s while maintaining the advantage of stable luminous intensity through multi-confinement design [28]. However, the interruption of phosphorescence and poor dispersion in water still limit their practical application in anti-counterfeiting and bioimaging [13,16,28]. Therefore, it is necessary to develop CDs-based RTP with good dispersion in water and maintaining intensive phosphorescence in aqueous environment. In addition, in order to expand the application of CD materials in anti-counterfeiting and information encryption, it is also important to further achieve the tunable phosphorescence color of the materials. So far, Liu’s group have made great progress in water-soluble RTP materials, they designed a CDs-silica nanoparticle composites for ultralong phosphorescence imaging, which could stably emit 1.57 s green phosphorescence in biological tissue [24]. Furthermore, it was found that CDs-based afterglow in aqueous environment could be also achieved by covalently linking with nano silica, zeolites, and MOFs [24,29–31] or by utilizing bound water to build a hydrogen bond network between CDs and cyanuric acid [32]. However, these kinds of carbon dots composites generally exhibit blue-green phosphorescence emission, which largely restrict their application in biological imaging and anti-counterfeiting. To gain multicolor RTP-CDs, Li’s group realized metal-free RTP with tunable color and an ultralong lifetime by B-doping [33], while Jiang’s group put forward three criteria to modulate the phosphorescence emission of single-component carbon dots: (1) Select substance containing abundant C = O/C = N functional groups as precursors. (2) Perform
n →
π
∗
transition favored heteroatom doping, which are deemed to play a role in immobilizing chromophores in carbon dots. (3) Use relatively higher reaction temperature, which is thought to help produce more compact structure. To validate these hypotheses, the group successfully synthesized a range of structurally similar single-component CDs with bule-to-yellow RTP emission. But unfortunately, such CD-based RTP materials have no ability to maintain the phosphorescence in aqueous environment [17]. Therefore, how to achieve efficient and color-tunable RTP-CDs in aqueous solution requires further work.

To address the issue that the phosphorescence cannot be maintained in aqueous environment, the FRET (Förster Resonance Energy Transfer) strategy, a light-emitting modulation method that has been used to construct polychromatic fluorescent materials successfully, has gained new attention. Previous study noted that efficient FRET process relies on enough overlap between donor’s emission spectrum and acceptor’s absorption spectrum, and the transfer rate
k t
varies inversely with the 6th power of the donor–acceptor distance (
r
6
) over the range of 1–10 nm [34,35]. Thus, we proposed that there should be three main factors to achieve phosphorescence-based FRET: (1) The emission spectrum of donor and the absorption spectrum of acceptor must substantially overlap. (2) Donor and acceptor should be confined close enough to enhance the efficiency of energy transfer. (3) Phosphorescent emitters have sufficiently high quantum yield and phosphorescence intensity to stimulate the emission of acceptors. Based on these, it should be a proper method to achieve multicolor phosphorescence in aqueous environment by designing a hydrophilic confinement system to combine CDs and corresponding illuminant [35].

According to previous work, amorphous silica could function as an ideal host for loading CDs and various fluorescent dyes to achieve energy transfer process in aqueous environment. In this study, we developed a simple and reliable synthetic strategy to synthesis silica-encapsulated RTP carbon nanodots with long tunable-lifetime, low toxicity, good luminescence stability and adjustable color in aqueous environment. By utilizing of chitosan as the carbon source and diethylenetriamine as the N-doping resource, a cross-linked skeleton was formed as the luminescent center, and then a rigid network was formed by silica encapsulation (CD@SiO2). The obtained CD@SiO2 not only possess good water solubility, but also show stable long lifetime phosphorescence (green, 503 nm, 1.10 s). In addition, by co-encapsulating CDs and proper organic dyes in SiO2, which serve as a confinement structure to enhance the energy transfer efficiency between CDs and dyes, the composite materials CD@SiO2@R6G and CD@SiO2@DCF have been obtained. These materials can also emit long lifetime phosphorescence (CD@SiO2@R6G: orange-yellow, 570 nm, 1.13 s; CD@SiO2@DCF: yellow-green, 530 nm, 1.20 s), achieving tunable phosphorescent color. The materials reported in this work have great application potential in multi-color anti-counterfeiting, multi-layer information encryption and biological systems. Besides, leverage on phosphorescence FRET process, we purpose a new strategy to regulate RTP materials’ phosphorescence lifetime, which is expected to be widely applied in lifetime-analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Research design and materials synthesis strategy
ce:section.ce:para: In this research, the luminescence center, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (CDs), were simply synthesized by hydrothermal method using quaternary ammonium salt chitosan as carbon source and diethylenetriamine as nitrogen source. SiO2 was used as encapsulation (CD@SiO2) to effectively excite the phosphorescent emission of CDs, which could build rigid networks and confine ISC process to achieve a water-soluble and long lifetime RTP CD material. In addition, the emission wavelength of CD@SiO2 has a large overlap with the excitation wavelength of some organic dyes (e.g., rhodamine 6G, 2′, 7′-dichloride fluorescence), enhancing the energy transfer process between CD@SiO2 and these organic dyes efficiently. Therefore, SiO2 was adopted as confinement structure and the organic dye-doped CDs were encapsulated in the same rigid space, which further enhanced the energy transfer efficiency between donor and acceptor. Eventually, a water-soluble, long lifetime, and phosphorescent color-tunable RTP material (CD@SiO2@R6G, CD@SiO2@DCF) was obtained (Fig. 1 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphological and structure characterization of CDs, CD@SiO2, CD@SiO2@R6G and CD@SiO2@DCF
ce:section.ce:para:
Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and High Resolution-Transmission electron microscope (HR-TEM) were used to analyze the morphology of CDs and CD@SiO2. HR-TEM image shows that the CD particles presented as a sphere with radius about 5 nm, and its interplanar spacing is about 0.22 nm, which is similar to that of graphene (001) lattice plane (0.21 nm) [36]. The slight difference between them may be caused by N doping [37] (Fig. 2
A, 2B). Thus, carbon dots may also form a graphene-like cross linked internal structure, which indicates that CDs are successfully embedded in silicon dioxide structure.

X-ray diffraction (XRD), Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) were used to evaluate the possible formation process and surface state of N-CDs. We found that CD@SiO2, CD@SiO2@R6G and CD@SiO2@DCF have similar diffraction peak shapes base on XRD data ( Figure S2 ), indicating that the three have similar SiO2 skeleton structures. From the FT-IR result of CD@SiO2 (Fig. 2 C), we observed a strong stretching vibration of Si-O-Si at 910 cm−1, which exhibited the existence of a large amount of spatial network composed of Si-O-Si; the broad band at 3500–3300 cm−1 was attributed to the existence of –OH, –NH2 on the surface of CDs; the intensity of the corresponding peak in CD@SiO2 decreased rapidly, which showed that the carbon dots were bonded to the silica through the surface free hydroxyl and amino groups; the peak at 754 cm−1 from Si-C indicated that CDs have been cross-linked with the Si-O-Si network through covalent bonds. Moreover, the peak of 1658 cm−1 was associated with C = N of the carbon core, which came from the dehydration and cross-linking process and served as the photoluminescence (PL) center. Simultaneously, XPS spectra also illustrated the functional groups of CD@SiO2. Basically, there are four major peaks for the XPS survey, indicating that CD@SiO2 consisted of C, N, O and Si elements (Fig. 2 E). Particularly, three peaks of the C 1s region were responsible for C–C (283.8 eV), C = N (284.5 eV), and C-N/C-O (285.6 eV), describing a large amount of C = N formed in the carbon core (Fig. 2 F). Likewise, the high-resolution O 1s spectra showed two peaks at 532.9 eV and 531.8 eV, which were related to Si-O (532.9 eV) and C-O (531.8 eV) bonds (Fig. 2 G). Similarly, the XPS N 1s bands of CD@SiO2 included C-N bonds at 399.3 eV, C = N bonds at 400.3 eV, and N-Si bonds at 401.7 eV (Fig. 2 H), and the C-N and C = N bonds demonstrated the cross-linking of CDs and nitrogen-containing groups during dehydration. Moreover, the XPS spectra of Si 2p exhibited two peaks at 102.5 eV and 103.9 eV (Fig. 2 I) derived from Si-C and Si-O bonds, respectively, demonstrating hydroxyl and Si-O networks on the surface of CDs.

This structure effectively limits the vibration and rotation of the chemical bonds inside the carbon dots, which in turn limits its nonradiative transitions [19]. Besides, the SiO2 shell structure prevents oxygen from contacting the light-emitting center, and finally prolongs the lifetime and enhances the stability of the material in the aqueous environment [24].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Luminescence properties of CDs, CD@SiO2, CD@SiO2@R6G and CD@SiO2@DCF
ce:section.ce:para:
Excitation, FL spectrum and FL spectra at different excitation wavelengths of CDs were explored to interpret the luminescence properties of the products. The FL emission presented a peak at 466 nm when excited at 364 nm (Fig. 3
A). While the FL spectra at different excitation wavelengths showed that, with the increase of excitation wavelength (335–395 nm), FL's peak location gradually redshifts (460–480 nm) (Fig. 3 B). According to red-edge effect, this excitation dependence phenomenon further confirms the graphene structure of CDs’ carbon core.

To further understand the optical properties of CD@SiO2, we analyzed the FL and Phos spectrum of CD@SiO2. The FL emission displayed a peak at 446 nm, while Phos peak located at 503 nm (Fig. 3 C), when excited at 345 nm (the optimal FL/Phos excitation wavelength of CD@SiO2). Compared with CDs, the excitation and fluorescence wavelength of CD@SiO2 display 20 nm red-shift, which may be credit to the coating of silica has changed the CDs’ surface functional groups.

To evaluate the luminescence stability of CD@SiO2, Phos intensity (Ex: 345 nm) of CD@SiO2 at different pH (3.0–11.0, set one group every 1.0), different atmosphere (No gas, continuously inlet N2/O2 for two hours), different temperature (Heat up or cool down to a certain temperature and then return to room temperature; testing temperature: −4 ℃, 0 ℃, 25 ℃, 70 ℃) and different duration of UV radiation (0 h, 1 h, 2 h, 4 h) were explored, respectively.

Furthermore, to achieve phosphorescent color tunability, we construct the composite material CD@SiO2@dye (CDs doped with organic dyes and then coated by SiO2). When excited by UV light at 345 nm, the composite material CD@SiO2@R6G had two peaks at 446 nm and 560 nm in the FL emission spectrum, corresponding to the two emission wavelengths of CD@SiO2 and rhodamine 6G at 345 nm, respectively (Fig. 3 D), which indicates that the mixing process did not change in the FL emission wavelength of the materials.

In addition, the phosphorescence spectrum was measured, which showed that a new emission peak appeared in 570 nm (Fig. 3 D). It is obvious that the new peak is derived from the added dyes. The R6G molecule has a wide absorption peak around 530 nm, therefore, the phosphorescence emission of CD@SiO2 has ability to stimulate the emission of R6G effectively. However, the new peak located at 570 nm presented a slight red shift compared to the free dye’s (560 nm). It may be credit to the confined structure which limited the rotation and vibration of dye molecules [41].

Plus, with the addition of R6G, the phosphorescence emission peak of the original CD@SiO2 at around 500 nm was significantly weakened, and the weakening degree increased when R6G concentration increased, while the new phosphorescence emission peak of R6G appeared at around 570 nm increased sharply. It demonstrated that there was an energy resonance transfer between R6G and CD@SiO2, and the energy transfer efficiency first increased with the increase of R6G concentration in 0.8 × 10−4 M – 1.4 × 10−4 M range (Fig. 3 F).

In summary, the phosphorescence emission wavelength of CD@SiO2@R6G can be adjusted step by step between 503 nm and 570 nm by changing the ratio of CD and R6G, and correspondingly, the color of the Phos emission gradually changes from green to yellow (Fig. 4
A), which achieves the tunable Phosphorescence color.

Moreover, to expand the range and means of regulating phosphorescence color, we further synthesized CD@SiO2@DCF (CDs doped with dichloride fluorescence and then coated by SiO2). Under the excitation light at 345 nm, the FL emission also displayed two peaks, at 446 nm and 520 nm (from CD@SiO2 and dichloride fluorescence), respectively, and the Phos peak is located at 530 nm (Fig. 3 E). The reasons for these spectral features may be similar to CD@SiO2@R6G.

Similarly, after the addition of DCF, the original CD@SiO2 at around 500 nm decreased and the new Phos emission peak of DCF at around 530 nm strengthened with the increase of DCF concentration within a certain range (DCF: 0.8 × 10−4 M – 1.7 × 10−4 M) (Fig. 3 G), proving the existence of the energy resonance transfer between DCF and CD@SiO2, and the energy transfer efficiency continuously enhances with the increase of DCF concentration. The Phos emission wavelength and Phos color of CD@SiO2@DCF can also be adjusted step by step (from 503 nm to 530 nm, from green to yellow) (Fig. 4 A) by changing the ratio of CDs and DCF.

To calculate the actual dye loading, the “UV absorption intensity - dye concentration” standard curves ( Fig. S3
B, S3D) were drawn based on the UV–Vis spectra of R6G and DCF at different concentrations ( Fig. S3
A, S3C). Select the product with the best luminescence effect for the UV–Vis spectrum test. According to the UV absorption intensity of the product and the standard curve, the dye loadings of the product with the best luminescence effect are 1.53 × 10−4 M (R6G, λm = 522 nm, the red dot in Fig. S3
B) and 1.8 × 10−5 M (DCF, λm = 502 nm, the red dot in Fig. S3
D), respectively.

The solution and powder of CD@SiO2 can emit blue fluorescence (365 nm UV excitation) and ultra-long lifetime (about 8 s by the naked eye) green afterglow (stop UV excitation), while the solution of CD@SiO2@R6G can emit orange-yellow fluorescence (365 nm UV excitation) and ultra-long lifetime (about 8 s by the naked eye) orange-yellow afterglow (stop UV excitation), and CD@SiO2@DCF can emit yellow-green fluorescence (365 nm UV excitation) and ultra-long lifetime (about 8 s by the naked eye) yellow-green afterglow (stop UV excitation) (Fig. 4 A, 5A).

The absolute phosphorescence quantum yields (PQY) of all compounds in the aqueous environment were measured with an integrating sphere under ambient conditions. The PQY of all samples were obtained following the equation where A and B represent the integral areas of total absorption (PL) spectra and phosphorescence spectra, respectively [30].
(1)
PQY =
B A
According to the above formula, the PQY of each product in aqueous can be obtained as follows: CD@SiO2: 1.36 %, CD@SiO2@R6G: 1.23 %, CD@SiO2@DCF: 1 %.

To explore the energy transfer relationship between materials, according to the formula (see in supplementary material), the fluorescence quantum yield (FQY) of each product in aqueous were obtained as follows: CDs: 12.02 %, CD@SiO2: 16.19 %, CD@SiO2@R6G: 38.48 %, CD@SiO2@DCF: 39.79 %.

In order to calculate the phosphorescence lifetime of materials, we have measured the phosphorescence decay curve of materials. First, to describe a decay process, the multiexponential PL decay function is as follows [42,43]:
(2)

Where Ai is the total amplitude and ai are the amplitude fractions.

The average PL lifetime can be calculated as:

Moreover, the photoluminescent lifetime of CD@SiO2@R6G and CD@SiO2@DCF are 1.13 s (Em = 505 nm; and 0.94 s, Em = 560 nm) and 1.10 s (Em = 500 nm; and 1.05 s, Em = 540 nm) (Table 1 ; Fig. 4 B, 4C), respectively, which means that these materials are soluble ultra-long room temperature phosphorescent materials.

According to above results, due to the high fluorescence quantum yield of dyes, the RTP of CD@SiO2@R6G and CD@SiO2@DCF will still show similar colors to dyes, which is significantly different from that of CD@SiO2.
Leverage on these materials and the CDs without phosphorescence, we can realize 4D information encryption based on time resolution [30].

In summary, these three materials have achieved high efficiency, long lifetime and color-adjustable phosphorescence emission in aqueous solution while maintaining the advantages of high stability, low toxicity, and simple synthesis process. Moreover, due to the phosphorescence property of materials and their different phosphorescence lifetime, we can simply achieve multi-dimensional encryption and anticounterfeiting based on color and time resolution.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Phos mechanism of CD@SiO2 and CD@SiO2@dye
ce:section.ce:para: To further understand the optical properties and investigate the origin of RTP, the UV–Vis spectrum of CD@SiO2 showed a large and distinct absorption band around 300 nm (Fig. 2 D), which could be attributed to the π-π* transition of the aromatic C = C and n-π* transition of C = N groups. The optimal FL/Phos excitation wavelength of CD@SiO2 is located around 345 nm, which largely overlapped with the corresponding absorption band around 300 nm in the UV–Vis spectrum. Therefore, the n-π* transition of C = N related groups was considered as one of the luminescence origins [17,44].

Additionally, the FL emission of CD@SiO2 largely overlaps with the phosphorescence emission (Fig. 3 C), revealing a small energy gap of around 0.3 eV between the lowest singlet state (S1) and the triplet state (T1) of CD@SiO2 [44,45]. This narrow gap could promote the intersystem crossing (ISC) to help accumulate the triplet excitons, thus facilitating the RTP of CD@SiO2. Moreover, the abondance Si-C and Si-O-C bonds could be observed in FT-IR and XPS spectrum (Fig. 2 C and 2I), which demonstrated that the carbon dot core strongly connected to the SiO2 shell through covalent bonds. These covalent bonds and rigid SiO2 structure could efficiently limit the rotation and vibration of the inner carbon core, which promotes the ISC process as well. In addition, the SiO2 network could protect the triplet state from quenching agents such as oxygen and moisture, which allows CD@SiO2 emitting persistently in aqueous environment [13,16,24,32].

Theoretically, the triplet excitons could accumulate through the n-π* transition originated from N atoms with lone-pair electrons. While during the current process, the n-π* transition of C = N played the role as the amine-based Phos center, whose electrons absorbed the photon at excitation wavelength and jumped from the ground state (S0) to the excited state (Sn). Next, the internal conversion process promoted the transition of Sn electrons to S1, and the FL of CD@SiO2 solution could be observed when S1 electrons returned to S0, whereas their phosphorescence could not be observed in aqueous solution due to the existence of quenching agent.

Taken together, we proposed a reasonable mechanism for phosphorescence of CD@SiO2. Initially, chitosan reacted with diethylenetriamine to form the covalently cross-linked frameworks, forming the Phos center which has a small energy gap between HOMO and LUMO. Then, the hydrogen bonds and covalent bonds between CDs and rigid silica structure (Si-C/Si-O-C) restricted the vibration and rotation of their surface groups and rigidified the whole system, which could be implicated by the variable temperature phosphorescence spectrum. Eventually, the excitons experienced the process of S1⋯T1 → S0 and thus boosted their RTP of CDs. Moreover, the silica network served as a protector to prevent triplet from quenching, which endowed materials the ability to emit phosphorescence in water.

Furthermore, considering the role that dyes play in the optical properties of materials, we proposed a reasonable mechanism for Phos of CD@SiO2@dye as well (Fig. 4 F). With UV ray as excitation light, the CD@SiO2 serve as luminescence center to generate T1 state electron, the energy gap between T1 and S0 is 2.47 eV, which is similar with S0-T1 energy gap of rhodamine and dichloride fluorescence. Thus, though FRET mechanism, the luminescence of CD@SiO2 excited the luminescence of dyes [34,35]. In other words, the CD@SiO2 serves as a stable repository of T1 electrons. When the excitation light is off, the electrons in this storage vault can be used as excitation source to excite the fluorescence of the dye. It shows the variable temperature phosphorescence spectrum of CD@SiO2@R6G between 283 K and 323 K in Fig. 4 D. Two emission peaks of CD@SiO2@R6G descends sharply accompanied by the rising of temperature, which further confirms the mechanism of phosphorescence. With the increase of temperature, the vibration and rotation of molecules intense, in the meantime, the probability of non-radiation dissipation enlarge as well, thus weakening the phosphorescence intensity of materials.

To illustrate the energy transfer process between the phosphorescent CDs and dyes, we investigate the photophysical properties of the donor (CDs) and acceptor (dyes). The energy transfer process is divided into two types, the most common form is radiation transfer process. Due to its weak emission and transfer efficiency, the radiation process is usually not the main role. As shown in the Fig. 4 E, the phosphorescence emission spectrum of CDs has greatly overlapped with the absorption spectrum of dyes, which leads to potentially efficient FRET pair. Based on these formulas, we calculated the value of R0 in CD@SiO2@R6G and CD@SiO2@DCF system, which are 2.12 nm (R6G) and 1.96 nm (DCF) respectively. Furthermore, we calculated the distance between the donor and the acceptor when the energy transfer efficiency of the system is the highest, which are 6.75 nm (R6G) and 5.88 nm (DCF) respectively. Unlike Dexter mechanism, which can only transfer energy in a short distance, Förster energy resonance transfer mechanism can still occur when the distance reaches 8 nm [34,35]. Thus, in our phosphorescence energy transfer system, the dominant process should be considered as FRET process.

Furthermore, the energy transfer efficiency can be described by phosphorescence lifetime ratio of pure donor and donor–acceptor composites. Therefore, this energy transfer process could also be used as a method to regulate the phosphorescence lifetime.

According to above method, we have calculated the distance between the donor and acceptor molecules, which are 7.35 nm (R6G) and 6.25 nm (DCF). The results are roughly consistent with the data calculated by emission intensity ratio, which means that the rationality of the calculation result is further proved. Therefore, we propose that phosphorescence FRET process could serve as a method to regulate the system’s emission lifetime. Compared with single fluorescent materials and common monochromatic RTP materials, the materials (CD@SiO2, CD@SiO2@R6G and CD@SiO2@DCF) reported in this paper have many advantages, such as tunable phosphorescent color, good dispersion in aqueous solution, long lifetime, high stability, and non-toxicity, which show a promising future in multi-color anti-counterfeiting and information encryption.

Specifically, a Chinese Calligraphy Brush Pen was used to dip the aqueous materials (CDs and CD@SiO2) and then draw kinds of patterns on the paper with the light-yellow color. These patterns showed bright blue fluorescence under UV; while when UV turned off, the patterns drawn by CDs no longer glowed, but the patterns drawn by CD@SiO2 showed bright green phosphorescent (Fig. 5
A, 5B). In addition, the materials reported in this paper also have tunable multi-color phosphorescence and different phosphorescence lifetime. Based on these, we achieved 4D encryption through these materials (Fig. 5 C), the letters “X”, “J”, “T” and “U” were drawn by CDs, CD@SiO2, CD@SiO2@R6G, CD@SiO2@DCF, respectively. Under the irradiation of UV lamp, “X” and “J” can emit blue florescence, and “T” and “U” can emit orange and green-yellow light, respectively. When UV is off, the letter “X” disappears rapidly while the “J”, “T” and “U” persistently emit green, yellow, and green-yellow phosphorescence, respectively, namely Info A. After turning off the UV lamp for two seconds, because of its shortest lifetime, phosphorescence of CD@SiO2@DCF goes out first, leaving pattern “J”, “T” as Info B. Similarly, when the phosphorescence of CD@SiO2@R6G goes out, the remained pattern “J” well become the Info C. Based on a predetermined coding rule, we can extract a new info D from these four groups of information, thus realizing the 4D encryption.

Chitosan quaternary ammonium salt (M.W. 10,000 95%) was purchased from Macklin. Diethylenetriamine (99%), Rhodamine 6G (99%) and Tetraethyl orthosilicate (98%) was bought from Aladdin. Deionized water was prepared by Vent Filter MPK01. Dichloro Flucrescein was bought from Shanghai Reagent No.3 Factory. Ammonia solution (25%) was bought from Tianjin Damao Chemical Reagent Factory. Acetic acid glacial (AR) was bought from Guangdong Chemical Reagent Engineering-technological Research and Development Center.

All the UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with a Lambd950 UV–Vis-NIF Spectrometer, the fluorescence and phosphorescence spectra were recorded with FLSPP20 Steady State and Transient State Fluorescence Spectrometer. Also, the high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) images were used to characterize the morphology of CDs and CD@SiO2 by using JEOL JEM-F200 (HR) Field Emission Transmission Electron Microscope and Talos F200X Lorenz Transmission Electron Microscope. The XPS survey of CD@SiO2 was achieved through an Thermo Fisher ESCALAB Xi + X-ray Photoelectron Spectrometer (XPS). Next, we analyzed the surface groups of CD@SiO2 by a Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrometer (Bruker V70). Also, a Bruker D8 Venture was used to acquire the X-ray powder diffraction (XRD). Finally, handheld UV lamp ZF-5 from Shanghai Jiapeng Technology Co., Ltd. was used to perform visual observation and take photos of application examples.

As a typical process, 30 mL deionized water, 0.06 gquaternary chitosan and 150
μ L
diethylenetriamine was added into a 50 mL centrifuge tube and evenly mixed. After microwaving for 30 min until the solution is clear, the solution was added into 50 mL Teflon hydrothermal reactor, which was heated at constant temperature in an oven at 240 ℃ for 12 h. Then the solution was cooled down to room temperature, the excess precursors and by-product were removed by dialyzing against water through a dialysis membrane (MWCO: 1000 Da) for 2 days to gain carbon dots solution (CDs solution).

Add 30 mL of CDs solution synthesized in the previous step and 120 mL deionized water into a three-necked flask and adjust the pH to 9.5 with ammonia. Then the solution was heated to 70 ℃ by a constant temperature magnetic stirrer. Then 2 mL TEOS was added into the flask when the temperature kept in 70 ℃ for more than 30 min. After keeping the reaction system at 70 ℃ for 5 h, the product solution was concentrated, centrifuged, washed twice with deionized water, and finally dispersed in 20 mL of deionized water to get CD@SiO2.

Add 30 mL of CDs solution synthesized in the previous step, 5 mL
10
- 3
M
dye solution (R6G, DCF) and 120 mL deionized water into a three-necked flask and adjust the pH to 9.5 with ammonia and acetic acid. Then the solution was heated to 70 ℃ by a constant temperature magnetic stirrer. Then 2 mL TEOS was added into the flask when the temperature reached 70 ℃ for more than 30 min. After keeping the reaction system at 70 ℃ for 5 h, the product solution was concentrated, centrifuged, washed twice with deionized water, and finally dispersed in 20 mL of deionized water to get CD@SiO2@dye.

In summary, multicolor water-soluble long-life room temperature phosphorescent materials were synthesized with a simple method. Briefly, quaternary ammonium salt chitosan reacted with diethylenetriamine to form the covalently cross-linked frameworks and the luminescence center, forming a small energy gap between HOMO and LUMO. After silica encapsulation, the formation of the hydrogen bonds and covalent bonds between CDs and silica (N-H⋯N/O-H⋯N/N-H⋯O) restricted the vibration and rotation of their surface groups and rigidified the whole system, thus boosting the RTP of CDs. Interestingly, excited by UV ray, the CD@SiO2 serve as luminescence center and generate T1 state electron, and the energy gap between T1 and S0 is 2.47 eV, which is similar with S0-T1 energy gap of rhodamine and dichloride fluorescence. Thus, though FRET mechanism, CD@SiO2@dye were obtained and tunable phosphorescent colors was achieved. In addition, the materials reported in this paper were also successfully applied in multi-color anti-counterfeiting and information encryption, which shows a promising future in the advanced image encryptions.
